critic_raw:
This is a well-constructed experimental design with genuine attention to leakage, confounds, and evaluation rigor. I will focus on the genuine methodological concerns rather than manufacture issues.

**Issue 1: The repository stratification and temporal split interact in a way that may be logically incoherent**

The design claims repositories are partitioned so that 70% appear only in training, 15% only in validation, and 15% only in test. It also claims a temporal split where training covers years 1–3.5, validation 3.5–4.5, and test 4.5–5+. These two constraints are simultaneously applied, but they can contradict each other. A repository active throughout all five years cannot appear in only one split under a temporal split — its PRs will exist across all time windows. The design does not clarify whether 'all PRs from the same repository appear in exactly one split' means (a) only repositories that are active in a single time window are eligible, (b) repositories are reassigned to the time window where most of their activity occurs, or (c) the temporal split is relaxed and repository assignment overrides time assignment. If (a), the design introduces survivorship bias by excluding long-lived repositories — which are likely the most important ones in an enterprise context. If (b), the temporal split guarantee no longer holds for repositories whose activity bleeds across windows. If (c), the temporal split guarantee is meaningless because labels for some training-set PRs may be generated by post-cutoff commits from the same repository. This is not a minor implementation detail — it is a fundamental logical tension in the two constraints. Evidence that this is a real problem: check what fraction of repositories in the dataset are active across more than one time window. In a five-year enterprise dataset, most repositories will be, making this a pervasive issue rather than an edge case.

**Issue 2: The BERT embedding model 'fit on training set messages only' is underspecified in a way that could silently leak temporal signal**

The design states the embedding model is 'fit on training-set messages only,' but does not distinguish between (a) using a frozen pre-trained model with no task-specific adaptation, (b) fine-tuning on training-set commit messages with an MLM or similar self-supervised objective, or (c) fine-tuning with defect labels as supervision. Case (a) has no leakage risk but also has no 'fit' to speak of — so mentioning 'fit on training set' implies (b) or (c). Case (c) is the obvious form of leakage: fine-tuning the encoder with label signal on training PRs means the 768-dim representation is shaped by defect outcomes, which is legitimate for training but must be verified to not include any gradient signal from validation/test labels. More subtly, case (b) risks vocabulary or semantic drift: if test-period commit messages use different terminology (new frameworks, new internal jargon), the training-fitted tokenizer/vocabulary may produce degraded representations for test data. This is a form of covariate shift specific to the text modality that the numeric baseline does not share, meaning AUPRC degradation in the text model could be incorrectly attributed to model failure rather than tokenizer mismatch. Evidence one way or the other: compare token out-of-vocabulary rates and embedding cosine similarity distributions between training and test commit messages. A large shift would indicate this is a real confound.

**Issue 3: The label definition creates asymmetric censoring that the temporal split does not fully resolve**

PRs merged late in the training window (e.g., month 42 of 42) have less than 180 days of observation before the cutoff when labels are generated. The design says the 180-day window is 'frozen before the experiment begins,' but does not clarify whether PRs merged in the last 180 days of the training period are (a) excluded, (b) labeled with partial observation (treating no-signal-yet as clean), or (c) labeled using post-cutoff bug signals. If (b), PRs merged near the training cutoff are systematically under-labeled as defect-prone, because their 180-day window has not elapsed. This means the training set has an artificially low defect rate near the cutoff compared to earlier periods, and any temporal features the model learns will be contaminated by this censoring artifact — the model may learn 'PRs near the training boundary look clean' for the wrong reason. If (c), this is outright label leakage. This same problem applies at the validation/test boundary. The design does not address this. Evidence: plot defect rate as a function of time-to-split-boundary. A systematic drop in defect rate near the boundary would confirm censoring contamination.

**Issue 4: The baseline's 'author history' feature creates a subtle form of train/test contamination under the repository stratification**

Author prior defect rate is computed from historical PRs. In the repository-stratified design where test repositories are held out, authors who contributed to both training and test repositories will have author history computed from their training-repository PRs. This means the baseline has access to author-level signal that effectively 'bridges' the train/test repository partition — an author's reputation learned in training repos is applied to test repos. This is not leakage in the strict sense, but it means the baseline benefits from a form of cross-repository transfer that the design claims to prevent. If the proposed model has no analogous mechanism (text embeddings are repository-agnostic by construction), then the comparison understates the baseline's advantage in author-rich test scenarios. Conversely, if the proposed model's text embeddings happen to capture author-specific stylistic signals (writing style in commit messages is often author-specific), the comparison unfairly advantages the proposed model on the author-generalization dimension. Evidence: ablate author history from the baseline and compare; separately, cluster commit message embeddings by author and measure within-cluster similarity to quantify author-style leakage into embeddings.

These four issues share a common root: the interaction between the two split dimensions (temporal and repository) is underspecified, and the label censoring problem is a direct consequence of that interaction. Issues 3 and 1 are the most severe because they can produce systematically wrong labels or a logically impossible partition. Issues 2 and 4 are real but more tractable.
defender_raw:
## Pass 1 — Analysis of Each Critique Point

### Issue 1: Logical incoherence between temporal split and repository stratification

**Critic's claim:** A repository active throughout all five years cannot appear in only one split under a temporal split. The two constraints contradict each other, and any resolution creates either survivorship bias, temporal split violations, or label leakage.

**Assessment:** This is a genuine and significant flaw. The design as written is ambiguous, and the critic has correctly identified that the two constraints interact in a way that cannot be simultaneously satisfied for long-lived repositories. The sentence 'All PRs from the same repository appear in exactly one split' is almost certainly false if a strict temporal split is also applied to a five-year dataset where most enterprise repositories are long-lived and continuously active.

**Counter-argument or concession:** I concede this point substantially. The design intended the repository stratification to operate *within* the temporal split — meaning each time window would have its own repository partition, not that a single repository would be globally assigned to one split. Under this interpretation, a repository active across all five years would appear in training (years 1–3.5), validation (3.5–4.5), and test (4.5–5+) time windows, but only its training-period PRs would be used for training, etc. The sentence 'All PRs from the same repository appear in exactly one split' was a poorly worded attempt to prevent repositories from appearing in multiple splits *within* the same time window, not across time windows. However, this is a serious drafting failure — the stated design literally contradicts itself, and the intended design (repository stratification *within* time windows) is a weaker constraint than what was claimed. More importantly, even under the corrected interpretation, the temporal isolation guarantee survives, but the claim that test repositories are 'never seen' repositories is no longer true for long-lived repos. The generalization claim must be restated: the model is evaluated on test-period PRs from repositories it has also seen in training periods. This is a meaningful degradation of the stated evaluation goal.

**What remains genuinely uncertain:** Whether the corrected interpretation (temporal split primary, repository stratification within windows as a secondary check) is sufficient to support the claims made. It likely is for temporal generalization but not for repository-level generalization as originally advertised.

---

### Issue 2: BERT embedding model 'fit on training set only' is underspecified and potentially leaky

**Critic's claim:** The phrase 'fit on training-set messages only' implies fine-tuning (case b or c), not just a frozen pre-trained model. Case (c) with label supervision is a leakage risk if not carefully managed. Case (b) risks covariate shift from tokenizer mismatch on test-period vocabulary. These are distinct failure modes that the design does not specify or address.

**Assessment:** The critic is largely correct that the description is underspecified. The phrase 'fit on training-set messages only' is ambiguous and the design should have been explicit about which of the three cases applies.

**Counter-argument:** For case (a) — frozen pre-trained model — there is no leakage and the 'fit on training set' language was simply imprecise. This is the most defensible interpretation and likely the intended one given the parenthetical '(pre-trained or lightly fine-tuned)'. For case (b), the critic is right that tokenizer vocabulary mismatch is a real covariate shift risk specific to the text modality, and that AUPRC degradation could be misattributed. However, this is a known limitation of using pre-trained language models on domain-shifted data, not a leakage problem. The appropriate fix is the diagnostic the critic suggests: OOV rate analysis and embedding distribution comparison between training and test commit messages. For case (c), label-supervised fine-tuning is legitimate if done on training data only with no gradient signal from validation or test — the design explicitly states preprocessing is fit on training only, which should cover this. However, the lack of explicit specification here is a real drafting gap. I concede that this point requires clarification in the design but does not represent a fatal flaw — rather, it requires one of three well-understood instantiations to be explicitly chosen and documented.

**What remains genuinely uncertain:** Whether the actual implementation (not described here) uses case a, b, or c; and whether OOV drift is material in the target dataset. This is empirically resolvable.

---

### Issue 3: Asymmetric label censoring near split boundaries

**Critic's claim:** PRs merged within 180 days of any split cutoff have incomplete observation windows. If labeled as clean by default (partial observation treated as no defect), the training set has systematically under-labeled defect-prone PRs near the cutoff. The model may then learn spurious temporal features correlated with this censoring artifact.

**Assessment:** This is a genuine and well-specified flaw. The design states the 180-day window is 'frozen before the experiment begins' but does not describe what happens to PRs that fall in the 180-day buffer zone before each cutoff. The critic is right that this creates a systematic defect rate drop near boundaries.

**Counter-argument or concession:** I concede this point. The standard solution — excluding PRs merged within the last 180 days of any training or validation period from those respective sets — is not described in the design. This is a meaningful omission. The 'frozen before experiment begins' language was intended to convey that label generation uses a fixed lookback rule, not that it handles boundary censoring. The fix is straightforward: create 180-day exclusion buffers at each split boundary and document this explicitly. Without this fix, the defect rate artifact is real, and the critic's diagnostic (plot defect rate as a function of time-to-boundary) would likely confirm it. This is a serious but fixable flaw.

**What remains genuinely uncertain:** The magnitude of the artifact depends on PR volume near the boundary and the typical defect rate. In a large enterprise dataset over five years, the boundary zone likely contains thousands of PRs, making this non-trivial in practice. The magnitude is empirically measurable but the existence of the problem is certain.

---

### Issue 4: Author history features bridge the repository partition in the baseline

**Critic's claim:** The baseline's author-history features are computed from an author's PRs across all training repositories. This means the baseline 'bridges' the train/test repository boundary for authors who contribute to multiple repos, while the proposed model's text embeddings may capture author style in a way that creates a parallel but undisclosed advantage. The comparison is therefore not cleanly controlled on the author-generalization dimension.

**Assessment:** This is a valid and subtle point. The author history feature as described is inherently cross-repository — it aggregates an author's entire history regardless of which repository it came from. This means the baseline partially violates the spirit of the repository-generalization evaluation, not via leakage but via a legitimate but asymmetric transfer mechanism.

**Counter-argument:** The critic is right that this undermines the clean interpretation of the repository-generalization test. However, this is an intended property of author history as a feature — it captures author reputation across the organization, which is precisely why it is a reasonable baseline for an enterprise setting. The question is not whether this is a flaw in isolation but whether it distorts the comparison. The critic identifies two directions of distortion: (1) baseline benefits from cross-repo author signal that the proposed model lacks, and (2) proposed model may have covert author-style signal in embeddings. Both of these are real ambiguities. The recommended ablation (remove author history from baseline; cluster embeddings by author) is the right diagnostic. I partially concede this issue: the design should have explicitly noted that author history is a cross-repository signal and that the comparison on repository generalization is therefore confounded on this dimension. The ablation is necessary to disentangle these effects.

**What remains genuinely uncertain:** Whether author-style signal in commit message embeddings is a meaningful confound depends on how stylistically distinctive individual authors are in commit messages — this is an empirical question specific to the dataset.

---

## Pass 2 — Verdict Selection

Reviewing my Pass 1 concessions:

- **Issue 1:** Substantial concession. The stated design is logically self-contradictory for long-lived repositories. The corrected interpretation weakens the repository-generalization claim materially. This is a critical flaw in the design as written.
- **Issue 2:** Partial concession. The underspecification is real but the failure modes are known and the fix is straightforward (choose and document one of three cases, add OOV diagnostics). Not a fatal flaw but a documentation gap.
- **Issue 3:** Full concession. The boundary censoring problem is real, certain to exist, and the design omits the standard fix (180-day exclusion buffer). This is a critical flaw that could produce systematic label artifacts.
- **Issue 4:** Partial concession. Real asymmetry in the comparison, resolvable by ablation but not currently addressed. Meaningful but not fatal.

Two of the four issues involve critical flaws — Issue 1 (logical incoherence in the split design) and Issue 3 (certain label censoring artifact). These are not resolvable by argument alone: Issue 1 requires the design to be revised and re-stated, and Issue 3 requires either a design fix (exclusion buffer) or empirical confirmation of magnitude. The calibration rule requires that multiple critical concessions cannot support a `defense_wins` verdict. The issues are real enough to require design revision (Issues 1 and 3 are fixable in principle), but the magnitude of their impact on final AUPRC comparisons requires empirical verification. I therefore assign `empirical_test_agreed` — the methodology requires repair on Issues 1 and 3 before the comparison is interpretable, and the magnitude of distortion from Issues 2 and 4 requires empirical measurement.
all_issues_raised: The temporal split and repository-level stratification are logically incompatible for repositories active across multiple time windows, which likely covers most long-lived enterprise repositories.

The BERT embedding model 'fit on training set only' is underspecified and may silently encode temporal leakage if fine-tuned with labels, or introduce covariate shift via tokenizer mismatch if self-supervised.
PRs merged within 180 days of the training cutoff are subject to asymmetric label censoring, producing a systematic artifact where the defect rate drops near the split boundary.
Author history features in the baseline bridge the train/test repository partition because authors contribute to multiple repositories, undermining the repository-generalization guarantee.
all_issues_adjudicated: The temporal split and repository stratification are logically incompatible as written: the claim that 'all PRs from the same repository appear in exactly one split' cannot hold for long-lived repositories under a strict temporal split, and the corrected interpretation materially weakens the repository-generalization claim.

PRs merged within 180 days of each split boundary are subject to asymmetric label censoring that the design does not address, producing a certain systematic artifact where the defect rate drops near the boundary and potentially corrupting temporal features the model learns.

The BERT embedding description is underspecified regarding whether a frozen pre-trained model, self-supervised fine-tuning, or label-supervised fine-tuning is used, leaving open both a covariate shift risk and a documentation gap, though the failure modes are known and tractable.

Author history features in the baseline inherently bridge the repository partition via cross-repository author reputation, creating an asymmetric comparison on the repository-generalization dimension that requires an ablation to disentangle but is not addressed in the design.
